medications.indication_rationale: Short tapering oral steroid for severe/extensive blistering ACD to abort the inflammatory cascade.
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g., 25 mg daily for ~5 days (as per source note; per prescriber)

medications.indication_rationale: Localised potent topical steroid to intact/eroded skin.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply cool damp compresses instead of scratching; do not pop the blisters (the roof protects the raw skin).
Wash gardening clothes, gloves and tools in hot soapy water to remove residual plant oils.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents ongoing re-exposure to the allergen.

patient_education_points.point: Your skin has reacted strongly to a plant oil where the weeds touched you. The steroids calm the reaction from the inside and outside, and it should settle over a week.
Don't pop the blisters, and wash all the gardening gear so you're not re-exposed.
If your face or mouth swells or you have trouble breathing, or the blister fluid turns to thick yellow pus with a fever — get emergency care.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution and steroid weaning; consider patch testing to confirm the allergen.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty, or purulent infection with fever — emergency care.

must_include_items: Diagnose plant allergic contact dermatitis from the linear pattern and screen for SJS/systemic involvement
Potent topical ± short oral corticosteroid and itch control
Safety-net for systemic allergy/SJS and secondary infection
acceptable_alternatives: Non-sedating vs sedating antihistamine at night
errors_of_commission: Escalating to ED for a localised ACD (over-triage)
Advising the patient to pop the blisters
Under-treating extensive blistering ACD with topical only
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires correct ACD management (allergen removal + steroids) with an SJS/systemic and secondary-infection safety-net; avoid over-triage for a localised exposure dermatitis.
